deck_summary_one_sentence: Bi-monthly PwC Strategy& Nigeria economic outlook analyzing how global economic trends (monetary tightening, debt, geopolitics, trade, GDP slowdown) are impacting Nigeria's foreign exchange market and recommending a sequenced policy response.

recurring_elements.value: Impact of Global Economic Trends on Nigeria's Foreign Exchange Market

color_palette.notes: Strong red-and-black palette typical of PwC Strategy& branding. Primary red used for chart highlights and section markers; accent for secondary emphasis; neutrals dominate body content. No green/blue used.

chart_styling.notes: Charts consistently use red primary with gray/black supporting colors. Most charts include an 'Insights' side callout. Source attribution citation appears as small text near chart base ('Source: NBS, CBN, PwC Analysis' pattern recurs).

layout_conventions.no_fly_zones_observed: Consistent top-band for titles (action-title style, ~1-2 lines). Consistent bottom-band for footer ('Impact of Global Economic Trends...' + 'October 2023' + page number). Right-side or side-callout consistently used for 'Insights' panels on data slides. Many bespoke layouts for content slides — multi-column matrices, framework diagrams, dashboards.

slides.title_text: Nigeria (Bi-monthly) Economic Outlook
slides.main_message: Cover slide: Nigeria (Bi-monthly) Economic Outlook — Impact of global economic trends on Nigeria's foreign exchange and the way forward (October 2023)

slides.title_text: Content
slides.main_message: Contents: 1) Macroeconomic Context, 2) Economic Outlook, 3) Impact of Global Economic Trends on Nigeria FX Market and Way Forward, 4) PwC Economics Services

Macroeconomic dashboard for Nigeria as of Sept 29 2023: GDP growth 2.5% (Q2), official FX rate ₦769/$, parallel FX ₦1,008/$, inflation 25.8%, MPR 18.75%, oil production 1.4mbpd, reserves $33.2bn, market cap ₦36.3tn

slides.title_text: Setting the context (1/2)
slides.main_message: Setting the context (Part 1/2): macroeconomic situation across Consumer & Demand, Macro Dynamics, Prices & Markets Development, and Regulatory & Policy Development — covering GDP growth, inflation, FX, fiscal measures, and tax reforms

slides.title_text: Setting the context (2/2)
slides.main_message: Setting the context (Part 2/2): monetary dynamics ($7B FX backlog), fiscal dynamics (public debt to $113bn), and continued regulatory policy development (Tertiary Education Tax raised to 3%, repealed tax benefits, 0.5% import levy)

slides.title_text: The rise in Nigeria’s public debt to N87.4trillion in Q2 2023 could be attributed to the impact of securitisation of ways & means, and the naira devaluation
slides.main_message: The rise in Nigeria's public debt to N87.4 trillion in Q2 2023 is attributed to securitisation of ways & means (59.1% of the rise) and the impact of naira devaluation (34.9% of the rise)

slides.title_text: The rise in all energy pressure points may further squeeze consumer’s purchasing power
slides.main_message: All energy pressure points (kerosene, PMS, diesel, LPG) have been deregulated and now track international oil prices; fuel subsidy removal drove PMS prices up >200%, further squeezing consumer purchasing power

slides.title_text: Slowdown of GDP growth in Q2 2023 to 2.5% was attributed to continued effect of naira redesign policy
slides.main_message: Q2 2023 GDP grew marginally to 2.5% (from 2.3% in Q1) due to lingering cash crunch effects and emerging effects of subsidy removal and FX liberalisation; PwC projects 2.8% growth in 2023 and 3% in 2024

slides.title_text: Information & communication sector is the only fast growing dominant sector
slides.main_message: Of Nigeria's economic sectors, Information & Communication is the only dominant sector that's fast growing (8.6%); Financial & Insurance grew 26.8% but is smaller; Transportation & Storage contracted -50.6% from PMS subsidy removal

slides.title_text: Economic context and outlook
slides.main_message: Synthesized economic outlook: key assumption drivers (consumer/demand, macro dynamics, prices/markets, fiscal, monetary) mapped to likely impacts on Growth (2.8% PwC projection), Investment (capital reallocation continues), Consumer Demand (slowing), Production (FX-pressured), Government Spending (debt-constrained), Net Exports (FX-constrained)

slides.title_text: Framing the relationships between global trends and Nigeria FX market
slides.main_message: Conceptual framework: how five global trends (monetary tightening, debt sustainability, geopolitical risk, weakening trade, slowing GDP growth) flow through local trends → FX market mechanisms → FX outcomes (stability, price evolution, flexibility to shocks)

slides.title_text: Key global trends
slides.main_message: Five key global trends summarized: 1) Monetary tightening (Fed/ECB rate hikes), 2) Debt sustainability concerns ($92T global public debt), 3) Heightened geopolitical risk (Russia-Ukraine, fragmentation), 4) Weakening global trade (China exports -8.8%), 5) Slowing GDP growth (US/EU/UK declining)

slides.title_text: The impact of the global transmission mechanisms on price discovery, liquidity, and friction in forex access in Nigeria
slides.main_message: Matrix mapping global transmission mechanisms to their specific impact on price discovery, liquidity, and friction in Nigeria's FX access

slides.title_text: The global financial landscape is more challenging (1/2)
slides.main_message: Global financial landscape more challenging (1/2): since US tightening started 18 months ago, EM dollar bond yields rose ~200bps, foreign currency bond issuance fell, and portfolio flows to major EMs sharply declined

slides.title_text: The global financial landscape is more challenging (2/2)
slides.main_message: Global financial landscape more challenging (2/2): nominal yields on 10-year US Treasury bonds have risen above 4%, reaching their highest point since the Global Financial Crisis

slides.title_text: Increase in interest rates may lead to reallocation of capital from frontier markets
slides.main_message: Higher interest rates (Fed funds 5.25-5.5%) drive capital reallocation from frontier to developed markets; Nigeria MSCI index outperformed frontier index 23.5% vs 1.4% but declined sharply 2020-2022 (-113% to -3.02%)

slides.title_text: Rising debt service to revenue ratio has led to the reallocation of capital away from the economy
slides.main_message: Nigeria's debt service to revenue ratio rose to 96% in 2022, triggering low credit ratings (Moody's, Fitch) and driving capital importation from $24B in 2018 down to $5.3B in 2022

slides.title_text: The prevalence of geoeconomic disintegration is on the rise
slides.main_message: Geoeconomic disintegration rising: ~3,000 trade restrictions imposed last year (3x 2019 levels) due to pandemic and Russia-Ukraine war; Nigeria highly exposed via wheat imports ($2.1B in 2022 = 4% of total imports)

slides.title_text: Decline in global trade growth may adversely affect Nigeria’s trade balance and FX inflows
slides.main_message: Global trade improved Q1 2023 ~2% YoY from China reopening, but UNCTAD predicts weak Q2 from inflation and Ukraine conflict; Nigeria's trade balance directly correlates with global trade growth and FX inflows are pressured

slides.title_text: Weakening global growth may decelerate remittance flows to the country
slides.main_message: Weakening global growth may decelerate remittance flows to Nigeria; global remittances to LMICs growing just 1.4% in 2023 (vs 8% in 2022); historical pattern shows -28% remittance drop during COVID and -5% in 2009 financial crisis

slides.title_text: WDAS/RDAS utilisation and FX intervention accounted for 70% of FX outflows between 2004 and 2021
slides.main_message: WDAS/RDAS utilisation and FX intervention accounted for 70% of all FX outflows 2004-2021; other official payments accounted for 15%; external debt service rose from 1% in 2015 to 37% in 2021

slides.title_text: 59% of annual FX inflows were from autonomous sources in 2022
slides.main_message: 59% of annual FX inflows came from autonomous (non-CBN) sources in 2022; since 2007 FX from autonomous sources exceeded CBN inflows, meaning official interventions don't reflect true market demand/supply

slides.title_text: Parallel market premium widened in 2023 with the rise in import bills and the decline in crude oil exports
slides.main_message: Parallel market premium widened to 28% in 2023 driven by import bill rises and crude oil export declines; CBN historically maintained official rate via sanctions but artificial strength caused non-tradable services to grow in import basket

slides.title_text: Total net flows through the CBN and autonomous sources has been declining
slides.main_message: Net flows through CBN are now negative, indicating declines in oil sector receipts and non-oil autonomous inflows; autonomous USD inflows peaked at $103B in 2013 and gap between official/parallel rates widened sharply in 2016 from $35/bbl oil price

slides.title_text: Trade-offs in exchange rate policy trilemma
slides.main_message: The exchange rate policy trilemma: a country can only choose two of three corners — capital mobility, pegged exchange rate, financial/monetary autonomy; capital controls and exchange flexibility trade off against each other

slides.title_text: Framing the relationships between global trends and Nigeria FX market
slides.main_message: Synthesis (same framework as slide 10) — relationships between global trends and Nigeria FX market with key analyses numbered 1-5, mapping supply dynamics, demand dynamics, and net flows back to the five global trends

slides.title_text:
Manage for flexibility and shocks – withstand external shocks with a
pocket of sequenced policy execution
slides.main_message: Way forward — five-step recommendation: manage for flexibility and shocks through sequenced policy execution covering short-term FX liquidity fix, deepening financial markets, boosting investor confidence with clear FX management story, and longer-term sectoral policy to maximize exports or deepen domestic consumption

slides.title_text: PwC Economics team proposition
slides.main_message: PwC Economics team service offering: (1) measure client impact on economy/competition/markets via EIA/SEIA/RIA/policy analysis, (2) help clients make investment/risk decisions via macroeconomic research and country/industry risk assessments, (3) help clients plan future macroeconomic and sustainability scenarios

slides.title_text: Contact Us
slides.main_message: Contact information for PwC Strategy& Nigeria Economics team: Olusegun Zacchaeus (Partner and West Africa Lead), Omomia Omosomi (Lead Economist & Researcher), Adesola Borokini (Senior Economist & Researcher)

provenance.confidence_notes: HIGH confidence on slide_purpose, message_type, main_message — action titles are explicit throughout, making content tagging straightforward. The deck is a textbook example of consulting action-title style. HIGH confidence on chart_type per slide based on context (most are bar/line/stacked bar; slide 5 inferred as waterfall from text describing debt buildup components). MEDIUM confidence on zones — inferred from text-block patterns; production system should parse actual shape positions. MEDIUM confidence on placeholder_compliance — most content slides marked 'Reusable' because they follow consistent title-band + main-chart + side-callout-insights pattern but use custom shapes not master placeholders. Slides 8, 9, 10, 24, 25 marked 'Bespoke' as they use unique conceptual diagrams. The design_system.title_style.size_pt of 15.0 from input extraction seems low for a primary title — likely captured a smaller text element; titles in body of deck appear larger. The body_style size of 7pt likely captured citation/footer text, not body paragraphs. Recommend humans audit these element-level rules. This is a Strategy& Nigeria publication (PwC's strategy practice), so reusability_score values reflect Strategy&-specific patterns, not general PwC. Deck shows clear 3-act structure: Context (1-9) → Analysis (10-25) → Recommendation + Capabilities (26-28).
